由陳雲提出

https://lihkg.com/thread/1493700/page/24
IATA Hong Kong,
Unit 1505, 15th Floor,
Sunlight Tower,
No.248 Queen’s Road East,
Wanchai, Hong Kong (SAR)
Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing an email to express my concern for Cathay Pacific Airways (IATA code:CX) is political repressed by Chinese authority.

Hong Kong has been under spotlight in the past two months for protesting against a proposed legislation, the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance, which potentially threatens local citizens and foreign travelers who are suspected of committing offence listed in this ordinance by Chinese Government. This proposed legislation allows transferring of any suspects to Mainland China.

Over two million of protestors in Hong Kong walked on the streets on demonstration and expressed their demands peacefully. Protestors also tried different measures such as strikes for voicing their opinions out. Cathay's crucial role came into focus much more recently. More than a thousand of its employees participated in a strike that forced the airline to cancel over 150 flights. Days later, Chinese Government forced Cathay Pacific CEO to give names of staff joining Hong Kong protest with a threat that does not allowing planes of Cathay Pacific flies over China. Yet CEO refused to do that. Cathay is solely grappling with threats from Chinese Government. As a result, Cathay had terminated two pilots in association with activities related to the protests and the company announced that CEO Rupert Hogg and Chief Commercial Officer Paul Loo were out on 16th Aug. The latest

According to the article 27 in Hong Kong Basic Law, Hong Kong residents shall have freedom of speech, of the press and of publication; freedom of association, of assembly, of procession and of demonstration; and the right and freedom to form and join trade unions, and to strike. Hong Kong and Chinese government ignored that article which entitles all Hong Kong citizens to have right to participate strikes. More terribly, Cathay Pacific continuous looking for staff who took part in protest and dismiss them under the threat of Chinese Government.

Aviation brings benefits to national and global economies. None of aviation companies should be threatened due to political reasons. IATA, as the largest representation of aviation industry, should stand out with the employees of Cathay Pacific. We sincerely wish you to condemn China’s interference that violates International Air Service Transit Agreement

Best wishes,
XXXXXXXX

Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing an email to express my concern for Cathay Pacific Airways (IATA code:CX) is political repressed by Chinese authority.

Hong Kong has been under spotlight in the past two months for protesting against a proposed legislation, the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance, which potentially threatens local citizens and foreign travelers who are suspected of committing offence listed in this ordinance by Chinese Government. This proposed legislation allows transferring of any suspects to Mainland China.

Over two million of protestors in Hong Kong walked on the streets on demonstration and expressed their demands peacefully. Protestors also tried different measures such as strikes for voicing their opinions out. Cathay's crucial role came into focus much more recently. More than a thousand of its employees participated in a strike that forced the airline to cancel over 150 flights. Days later, Chinese Government forced Cathay Pacific CEO to give names of staff joining Hong Kong protest with a threat that does not allowing planes of Cathay Pacific flies over China. Yet CEO refused to do that. Cathay is solely grappling with threats from Chinese Government. As a result, Cathay had terminated two pilots in association with activities related to the protests and the company announced that CEO Rupert Hogg and Chief Commercial Officer Paul Loo were out on 16th Aug. The latest

According to the article 27 in Hong Kong Basic Law, Hong Kong residents shall have freedom of speech, of the press and of publication; freedom of association, of assembly, of procession and of demonstration; and the right and freedom to form and join trade unions, and to strike. Hong Kong and Chinese government ignored that article which entitles all Hong Kong citizens to have right to participate strikes. More terribly, Cathay Pacific continuous looking for staff who took part in protest and dismiss them under the threat of Chinese Government.

Aviation brings benefits to national and global economies. None of aviation companies should be threatened due to political reasons. IATA, as the largest representation of aviation industry, should stand out with the employees of Cathay Pacific. We sincerely wish you to condemn China’s interference that violates International Air Service Transit Agreement

Best wishes,
XXXXXXXX

Alexandre de Juniac became the seventh person to lead the International Air Transport Association (IATA) when he took on the role of Director General and CEO from 1 September 2016.
De Juniac has almost three decades of experience in both the private and public sectors. This includes senior positions in the airline and aerospace industries and the French government.
De Juniac served as Chairman and CEO of Air France-KLM (2013-2016) and prior to that as Chairman and CEO of Air France (2011-2013). Under de Juniac’s leadership Air France and Air France-KLM underwent a successful restructuring that improved efficiency and strengthened performance. He has also served on the IATA Board of Governors (2013-2016).
De Juniac has broad aviation sector experience, including 14 years at French aerospace, space, defense, security and transportation company Thales, and its predecessor companies Thompson-CSF and Thompson SA (1995-2009). In his last position at Thales, de Juniac was responsible for the company’s operations and sales in Asia, Africa, the Middle East and Latin America.
De Juniac has also held positions in the French government. His career began with the Conseil d’Etat (State Council) from 1988 to 1993. Subsequently, he served in the Department of Budget (1993-1995); and in the Ministry of Economy, Industry and Employment as Chief of Staff to then Minister Christine Lagarde (2009-2011).
A French citizen, de Juniac was born in 1962. He is a graduate of the Ecole Polytechnique de Paris and Ecole Nationale de l’Administration. At IATA he works from both the association’s main offices in Montreal, Canada and Geneva, Switzerland.

亞歷山大·德·朱尼亞克於 2016 年 9 月 1 日起就任國際航空運輸協會理事長兼首席執行官，成 為該協會的第七任領導人。

德·朱尼亞克在私營企業和公共部門有近 30 年的工作經驗。他曾在航空公司、航空航天業和法 國政府擔任高級職位。

德·朱尼亞克曾在法航-荷航集團擔任主席兼首席執行官(2013 年-2016 年)，在此之前他曾擔 任法國航空主席兼首席執行官(2011 年-2013 年)。他領導法航和法航-荷航集團成功改組，效 率提升，業績亦得到改善。他還曾在國際航空運輸協會理事會任職(2013 年-2016 年)。

德·朱尼亞克有著豐富的航空業從業經驗，曾在法國的航空航天、安防和運輸公司 Thales 及其前 身 Thompson-CSF 和 Thompson SA 工作 14 年(1995 年-2009 年)。在離開 Thales 前，他負 責公司在亞洲、非洲、中東和拉丁美洲的運營和銷售。

德·朱尼亞克還曾在法國政府任職。1988 年-1993 年，他在法國最高行政法院任職。之後，他曾 在預算部任職(1993 年-1995 年);他還曾在經濟、產業和就業部擔任克里斯蒂娜·拉加德部長 (Christine Lagarde)的首席顧問(2009 年-2011 年)。

德·朱尼亞克是法國人，生於 1962 年。他畢業於巴黎綜合理工學院(Ecole Polytechnique de Paris)和法國國家行政學院(Ecole Nationale de l’Administration)。在國際航空運輸協會，他 將在位於加拿大蒙特利爾和瑞士日內瓦的兩個主要辦公室工作。

Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing an email to express my concern for Cathay Pacific Airways (IATA code:CX) is political repressed by Chinese authority.

Hong Kong has been under spotlight in the past two months for protesting against a proposed legislation, the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance, which potentially threatens local citizens and foreign travelers who are suspected of committing offence listed in this ordinance by Chinese Government. This proposed legislation allows transferring of any suspects to Mainland China.

Over two million of protestors in Hong Kong walked on the streets on demonstration and expressed their demands peacefully. Protestors also tried different measures such as strikes for voicing their opinions out. Cathay's crucial role came into focus much more recently. More than a thousand of its employees participated in a strike that forced the airline to cancel over 150 flights. Days later, Chinese Government forced Cathay Pacific CEO to give names of staff joining Hong Kong protest with a threat that does not allowing planes of Cathay Pacific flies over China. Yet CEO refused to do that. Cathay is solely grappling with threats from Chinese Government. As a result, Cathay had terminated two pilots in association with activities related to the protests and the company announced that CEO Rupert Hogg and Chief Commercial Officer Paul Loo were out on 16th Aug. The latest

According to the article 27 in Hong Kong Basic Law, Hong Kong residents shall have freedom of speech, of the press and of publication; freedom of association, of assembly, of procession and of demonstration; and the right and freedom to form and join trade unions, and to strike. Hong Kong and Chinese government ignored that article which entitles all Hong Kong citizens to have right to participate strikes. More terribly, Cathay Pacific continuous looking for staff who took part in protest and dismiss them under the threat of Chinese Government.

Aviation brings benefits to national and global economies. None of aviation companies should be threatened due to political reasons. IATA, as the largest representation of aviation industry, should stand out with the employees of Cathay Pacific. We sincerely wish you to condemn China’s interference that violates International Air Service Transit Agreement

Best wishes,
XXXXXXXX

Hong Kong has been under spotlight in the past two months for protesting against a proposed amendment to the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance under which people wanted for crimes in mainland China could potentially be sent there to face trial, depending on the decision of the Beijing appointed Chief Executive of Hong Kong.

As a UN specialized agency to manage the administration and governance of the Convention on International Civil Aviation, ICAO should investigate and intervene the political oppression that Cathay Pacific is suffering from. No airlines should be punished or threatened by any regimes merely because of their employees exercised their right to protest, right to strike, right to assemble, and right to express.

Best wishes,
XXXXXXXX

replies.msg:
Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing to express my concerns on Cathay Pacific Airways (IATA code: CX), which is currently under political pressure from the Chinese authority.

In the past two months, Hong Kong has been under the spotlight for protesting against proposed legislation, the Fugitive Offenders Ordinance, which will potentially threaten not only local citizens but foreign travelers. Once one is suspected of committing an offence listed in this ordinance by the Chinese Government, the proposed legislation allows transferring of any suspects to Mainland China without any judicial procedures and judgments, which strongly damage the common law system and the rule of law in Hong Kong, especially the principle of presumption of innocence.

Other than the well-known two-millions demonstration, protestors also exerted different means to express their opinion and ask the government to respond the public demands, including strikes, non-cooperation actions, human chain as so. During the movement, Cathay Pacific has played a crucial role. More than a thousand of its employees participated in a general strike to urge the government to face the public and the public demands.

Yet, after the general strike, the Civil Aviation Administration of China, in the name of security concerns, forced Cathay Pacific CEO Rupert Hogg to submit a list of those participated in protests and published speech against HKSAR and China, otherwise their flights will be prohibited to fly over China's airspace. Other than the official prohibition of access to China's airspace, official media like Global Times and state enterprises/banks have criticized Cathay Pacific for not enough patriotism and downgraded Cathay's credit rating. Although Rupert Hogg and Chief Commercial Officer Paul Loo decided to bear full responsibility and resign to respond the demands from China on 16th Aug, the suppression and purge have still not yet come to an end. For instance, Cathay Pacific terminated two pilots in association with activities related to the protests, one pilot with a charge of riot but not yet judged as guilty, a pilot who only said "Hongkoners Stand Strong" during landing announcement, and a flight attendant who is also the chair of union due to speeches supporting protests on social media platform.

According to the article 27 in Hong Kong Basic Law, Hong Kong residents shall have freedom of speech, of the press and of publication; freedom of association, of assembly, of procession and of demonstration; and the right and freedom to form and join trade unions, and to strike. Hong Kong and Chinese government ignored that articles which entitle all Hong Kong citizens to have the right to strikes. More terribly, Cathay Pacific is continuously searching for staff who took part in protests and dismiss them under the threat of the Chinese Government.

It is not an internal issue but an international alert. Once the Chinese government succeed to have full control of Cathay Pacific and starts a precedent, it means that China could easily intervene and manipulate business decisions and infringe the rights and safety of foreign aviation-related practitioners.

To sustain the prosperity of aviation sector which brings enormous benefits to national and global economies, ICAO shall take immediate action against China who attempted to violate the international conventions and rules, and intervene the business decision, thus the liberal market economy system. No aviation companies shall be threatened by political suppression. IATA, as the largest representation and international authority of the aviation industry, should stand with the employees of Cathay Pacific in response to China's brutal intervention on the international aviation sector.

We sincerely wish you to not only condemn China’s interference that violates International Air Service Transit Agreement, but also take immediate and substantive actions over China's misconduct.

Best wishes,
XXXXXXXX
